1.label: Question 1
1.scenario:
Company A is a newly established health and care insurance company operating in Country X. Company A specialises in individual Private Medical Insurance (PMI) products. Company A's existing PMI products provide comprehensive cover, where full reimbursement is provided for all medical costs incurred in hospitals and for any other treatments covered by the product. These are one-year annually renewable products.
Individual PMI policyholders in Country X were previously able to claim tax relief on their premiums. This tax concession was abolished by Country X's government just over a year ago, which resulted in a reduction in Company A's new business volumes.
Company A has now decided to launch a new budget PMI product to help increase its new business.

i.question: Discuss the possible advantages and disadvantages of adding a budget PMI product to its product range from Company A's perspective.

Indicative Solution:
Advantages to Company A:
Offering a budget PMI product can help insurers tap into a previously underserved market segment.
It may stop overlapping of benefits with the customer already had for say under employer-employees scheme or government benefits.
Many potential customers may be looking for more affordable healthcare coverage options: and a budget PMI product can attract these price-conscious individuals post tax abolition.
Providing a more affordable option, the insurer can stand out among competitors and gain a larger market share.
Expanding the product range with a budget PMI product diversifies the insurer's portfolio.
Existing policyholders who may be considering switching to a more affordable provider could be retained if the insurer offers budget PMI as a cost-effective alternative within the same company.
Attracting a new customer base and retaining existing ones can lead to increased premium revenue, and assuming this business was written profitability it will increase the overall profits of the company, even if the per policy profit/pricing margins are lower on budget policies (as long as this is true new business, not a redirection from the higher value policy).
Offering a lower-tier PMI product, the insurer can cater to a broader range of customers with varying health needs; and potentially reducing the risk associated with high-cost claims.
Increased business volumes allows overhead expenses to be spread over a wider number of policies, potentially leading to higher economies of scale / expense efficiency.

Disadvantages to Company A:
Risk that policyholder does not realise at the time of purchase that budget PMI products will have less cover than full PMI product, which could lead to customer dissatisfaction / complaints / reputational damage against Company A.
Budget PMI products typically come with lower premiums (but similar fixed costs) and therefore offer thinner profit margins compared to standard PMI policies.
Offering budget PMI may attract a riskier customer base who are more likely to make claims. This can lead to adverse selection, where the insurer has to pay out more in claims relative to the premiums charged.
Managing a diverse portfolio of insurance products, each with its own pricing, coverage, and underwriting criteria, can increase administrative complexity, and administrative costs.
It may be difficult to find a reinsurer for these budget products.
Offering budget PMI may risk diluting the insurer's brand image if the product is seen as lower quality or lacking comprehensive coverage.
Insurers need to ensure that budget PMI products meet regulatory standards and provide adequate coverage to policyholders, which can be challenging at lower price points.
The insurer's marketing and distribution channels may not be suitable for the budget PMI customer segment, additional costs will be incurred if new marketing and distribution channels need to be established.
Company A will need to invest in increased systems capabilities. If Company A does not sell enough new business, then it will not recoup these development costs. On the other hand, if too much new business is sold, this could incur excessive capital strain.
Budget PMI may come with limitations on the choice of healthcare providers and hospitals and can affect customer satisfaction and retention.
Possible product “cannibalisation” - this could take business away from the more profitable variant within Company A’s existing product range.

ii.question:
The new budget PMI product proves to be successful in increasing new business. Company A's Marketing Director has proposed adding the following features to the budget PMI product to help boost new business sales further:

• Increasing the maximum entry age from 65 to 75
• Extending the policy term from one year to three years, where premiums are guaranteed throughout the three-year term.

Assess the suitability of the Marketing Director's proposal.

Indicative Solution:
Increasing the maximum entry age under a budget Private Medical Insurance (PMI) product:
Arguments for:
Raising the maximum entry age can expand the potential customer base. It allows older individuals to purchase the policy, which may be appealing to those who may have previously been excluded due to age limitations.
Budget product with a lower premium may be more affordable to the older and retired individuals.
Increasing the maximum entry age could enhance insurer's brand name / image as it could be perceived by the public as more inclusive.
With more individuals eligible to purchase the policy, the insurer can potentially generate additional profits especially from older customers who are likely to pay higher premiums.
Expanding the customer base to include older individuals can help diversify the risk profile of the insurance portfolio as older policyholders may have different healthcare needs and risk factors (e.g. geographical concentration / socio-economic groups).
Existing policyholders who reach the previous maximum entry age may be more likely to renew their policies; or purchase additional coverage if they know they can continue with the same insurer.

Arguments against:
Allowing older individuals to enter the policy can lead to higher claims costs. Older individuals tend to have more healthcare needs and are more likely to make claims, which can strain the insurer's financial resources.
Pricing a budget PMI product for older customers can be challenging. Because there may not be sufficient credible data for Company A to assess and price the risk accurately.
Underwriting and claims management may also need to be updated which would incur additional costs.
Older lives may have more complex health issues / a lot of pre-existing conditions and underwriting could be more costly than for younger lives.
Premiums may need to be higher, which could deter potential customers looking for low-cost options. This could lead to anti-selection.
Increasing the maximum entry age may subject the insurer to additional regulatory scrutiny and compliance requirements.
Individuals who are over 75 may be disappointed that the maximum entry age does not extend beyond 75, which could adversely affect Company A's brand image.
The product may not attract sufficient customers over the age of 75 if PMI is being regarded as non-essential insurance in Country X. If this happens, Company A may find it difficult to recoup the development costs.

Extending the premium guarantee under a budget Private Medical Insurance (PMI) product:
Arguments for:
Extending the premium guarantee can provide policyholders with more certainty and stability in their insurance costs, which can enhance customer satisfaction and improve retention / lapse / persistency experience.
A longer premium guarantee can be a selling point for the insurer, making their budget PMI product more attractive to customers compared to competitors with shorter guarantee periods. This can be particularly effective in markets where price stability is a significant concern.
Enhanced customer satisfaction will also help improve Company A's brand name / image.

Arguments against:
Higher premiums may need to be charged for the cost of guarantee which could have adverse effect on new business sales.
Alternatively, extending the premium guarantee may require the insurer to adjust profit margins to account for the increased risk of healthcare inflation and other cost factors.
In a competitive insurance market, extending premium guarantees may limit the insurer's ability to adjust pricing in response to changing market conditions or underwriting risk. This could adversely impact profitability.
Lack of morbidity experience data with extended guarantee at higher ages may lead to higher pricing risk to the insurer - which could impact premiums.
Reinsurance risk may arise as suitable reinsurance may not be available for these extended benefits.
Expertise may not be available to price this new product especially for cost of guarantee as a result of extending policy term beyond one year.
The longer guarantee period will lead to higher reserving and capital requirements, which could lead to additional investment risk relating to assets that are used for backing the increased level of liability.
Insurers may need to navigate regulatory requirements and oversight when offering extended premium guarantees, which can be complex.
The changes in product design may lead to additional IT system/expertise cost to include these features for valuation.
Risk of lapse and re-entry if the premium/price falls over the period of the guarantee.

2.label: Question 2
2.scenario: In Country Y, all health and care insurance products have historically been provided solely by Company B, a state-owned health and care insurance company. The government of Country Y recently privatised Company B. Company B is now wholly owned by a large multi-industry company, and the government of Country Y has opened up its heath and care insurance sector to other private investors. In order to operate in the newly opened heath and care insurance sector, investors will need to set up a health and care insurance company in Country Y and apply to the insurance regulator of Country Y for authorisation.

i.question: Suggest possible reasons why the government of Country Y decided to privatise Company B and allow new health and care insurance companies to operate in Country Y.

Indicative Solution:
Reasons for the government to consider:
Privatising Company B:
Financial Reasons:
Governments may want to privatise public health insurers to reduce the financial burden on government of managing such an organisation.
Privatisation could lead to cost savings and shift the financial responsibility to the private sector.
The government could raise money by selling Company B to the private sector, and will benefit from tax on the future profits of the company.
The introduction of competition in the insurance market through privatisation could encourage greater efficiency and innovation.
Private insurers may offer better services and cost-effective solutions compared to a government owned monopoly.

Political Reasons:
This could be the result of a political promise by Country Y's government, as part of their political ideology or belief in limited government intervention.
The governments may believe that healthcare administration and insurance are not their core functions.
Privatisation could allow government resources to focus on regulating the provision of healthcare services while leaving private insurers to manage the provision of healthcare services.
In cases of fiscal deficits or economic challenges, the government may see privatisation as a way to help improve the government budget.
Privatisation could lead to a reduction in bureaucratic processes and government intervention, streamlining the healthcare system and improving overall effectiveness.
Privatisation could attract private investment in the healthcare sector, stimulating economic growth and job creation.

Other Reasons:
Private insurers may be motivated to improve the quality of service and offer more customer-centric solutions to attract and retain policyholders.
Privatisation may help reduce opportunities for corruption and favouritism within the public health insurance system.
Enhanced delivery of services may improve the health of the nation.
The government may have seen this work successfully in other countries.

Allowing new Health and Care Insurers to enter Country Y and selling insurance product:
Enhanced Customer Benefits:
Encouraging new entrants promotes competition in the insurance market, which could lead to more competitive pricing, improved services, and greater choices for consumers.
New insurers may offer a wider range of insurance products, catering to diverse customer needs and preferences. This could enhance customer satisfaction.
New insurers may introduce innovative insurance products, technology-driven solutions.
Competition among insurers could result in more affordable insurance options, making coverage more accessible to a larger segment of the population.
New insurers may specialise in niche products, offering coverage for specific health and care needs that may not be adequately addressed by Company B.
A competitive market empowers consumers to make informed decisions about their insurance, leading to better outcomes and value for money.

Economic Growth:
The presence of new insurers could stimulate economic growth by creating more job opportunities, encouraging investment, and fostering a competitive financial services sector.
Allowing new insurers may encourage the development of a local insurance industry sector, creating investment and business opportunities for companies in other industry sectors.
Insurers may work with healthcare providers to improve access to care, leading to a more robust/reliable healthcare system.

Other Reasons:
As Country Y's government could now focus on regulating the provision of insurance services, this could lead to enhanced regulatory oversight.
Enhanced regulatory oversight could lead to an improvement in the overall stability and trustworthiness of the insurance sector.
New insurers may leverage technology and data analytics to streamline processes, encouraging further development in the data analytic and IT industry sector.

ii.question:
Bank C is a large, established banking group in Country Y, providing a wide range of financial services, including current and savings accounts, mortgages, loans and credit cards, to a significant proportion of Country Y's population. Bank C's management have decided to enter the health and care insurance sector by setting up a new health and care insurance company, which will specialise in selling Critical Illness (CI) products.

Discuss the advantages and disadvantages for Bank C of entering Country Y's health and care insurance sector to specialise in selling CI business.

Indicative Solution:
Advantages to Bank C:
Bank C could increase its overall profits by selling CI business.
Selling Critical Illness insurance allows the bank to diversify its product range/revenue sources beyond traditional banking services. This could help provide stability in times of economic downturns.
Bank C could leverage their existing customer base to cross-sell Critical Illness insurance. Customers may find it convenient to purchase insurance from their trusted financial institution. This is particularly the case when the customers are taking out new loans and mortgages and seeking CI cover.
Offering additional financial products like insurance, the bank can deepen its relationship with customers. The enhanced customer journey/experience could lead to improved brand loyalty and customer retention.
Entering the insurance market allows the bank to reach a broader consumer base, including those who may not have previously used its services, which could help improve the bank's image/brand / reputation.
Offering Critical Illness insurance can help customers manage the financial risks associated with severe illnesses, which aligns with the bank's mission of safeguarding their financial well-being.
In some cases, banks may find synergies between their existing regulatory and compliance processes for financial services and insurance products, making it easier to enter the insurance market.
The business could help the bank to achieve a greater level of economy of scale by using existing infrastructure for insurance business.
If the design of solvency requirements is similar between banks and insurers, Bank C may be well placed to understand and implement the necessary operational process to meet the regulatory requirements.
There may even be solvency capital synergies as a result of any potential diversification benefits between banking and insurance products.
If other banks also write/sell insurance products, then Bank C needs to do the same to maintain their competitive position.

Disadvantages to Bank C:
Selling insurance products involves complex regulatory requirements that Bank C may not currently be familiar with. Complying with these regulations could be challenging and time-consuming, which could cause resource and financial constraints to Bank C.
More specifically, the level of regulatory capital required to support solvency could be significant.
Bank C may lack the expertise in underwriting, claims management, and actuarial sciences required for successful insurance operations, potentially leading to operational inefficiencies.
The recruitment of insurance specialists/staff could be a long drawn and costly process.
The insurance industry is highly competitive, and banks entering this space may struggle to compete with well-established insurance companies such as Company B and other new entrants with extensive product offerings and experience.
If the bank offers insurance products that do not meet customers' expectations or encounter issues in claims processing, it can damage the bank's reputation and erode customer trust. This is particularly the case for CI in terms of the precise definition of critical illness (e.g. Cardiac Arrest vs Heart Attack) and which critical illness to cover.
There will be significant development costs for Bank C; for example: IT / Admin systems and processes; Product design and pricing; Underwriting / Claims management; Sales and marketing; Actuarial and Financial systems and processes.
Selling health insurance products typically involves high distribution and sales expenses, which can increase new business strain compared with banking products.
Specialising in CI may not meet all the insurance needs from customers, for example, customers may consider death benefits / life covers to be more important or essential than CI cover.
Availability of suitable reinsurance - the bank will need to source reinsurance, which may be challenging.

iii.question:
The regulator of Country Y has requested that Bank C includes a three-year new business plan, together with solvency projections, as part of its application for authorisation.

Outline the process and relevant factors that Bank C should consider in preparing its three-year business plan and solvency projections.

Indicative Solution:
Bank C's health and care insurance company's three-year business plan and solvency projections information pack:
Process
The business plan will need to include business and product information to enable the regulator to understand Bank C's business propositions.
The financial information including the projected solvency positions over the next 3 years is effectively the projected balance sheets of Bank C's health and care insurance company, which will include the projections of assets, liabilities, and capital requirements.

Relevant factors and information to be considered would include:
Process related factors:
Consider seeking external input from actuaries, financial advisors, and legal experts to enhance the business plan production process.
Conduct internal and external independent review of the business plan before submission to the regulator.

Business and product information:
The range of CI products and services to be offered, including product features such as critical illness definitions and premium guarantees of the CI products.
Underwriting and claim management standards.
Pricing and business strategies.
Target market and customer segments.
The health and care insurance company's business and management structure, and where it sits in the overall group organisational structure of Bank C.
Assess market trends, competition, and regulatory environment.
Identify potential growth opportunities and market challenges.

Solvency Projections - New Business Volumes and Mix:
Bank C's assessment of CI market trends and competition, including any identified potential growth opportunities and market challenges to support the projected new business assumptions in the business plan.
Consider various financial scenarios, such as base case, pessimistic and optimistic new business scenarios to assess the range of potential outcomes.
Allow for the business mix and any cross subsidies likely to be present in the pricing strategy. This may be by demographic factors or policy size/sum assured.

Operational factors:
Overview of the operational processes, systems, and technology infrastructure.
Information relating to distribution channels, underwriting, claims processing, and customer service procedures.
Overview of the health and care company's business recovery plan.

Risk Information and Assessment:
Identify and evaluate potential risks that the insurer may face, including insurance, market / investment, tax and operational risks.
Management's risk management strategies, risk appetite and mitigation plans.
Health and care insurance company's reinsurance strategy.
Health and care insurance company's investment strategy.
Health and care insurance company's capital policy, including Bank C's initial capital injections and ongoing parental capital supports.
Health and care insurance company's dividend policy.

Solvency Projections - Assumptions:
Analysis and explanation to support demographic and financial assumptions made in the projections, including: claims experience; commission/expenses; tax; investment returns.
Consider stress scenario testing to assess sensitivity of solvency ratio to deviations in assumptions.
Consider reverse stress scenario testing to assess the potential stresses that could to the insolvency of the health and care insurance company.

Solvency Projections - Reserving and Capital Requirements:
Calculate the solvency capital requirement (SCR) based on the insurer's risk profile and regulatory requirements.
Determine the available solvency capital and evaluate the adequacy of solvency for the security of policyholders.
Ensure that the calculations align with the regulatory framework and requirements in the target market.

3.label: Question 3
3.scenario:
Company D is a newly established health and care insurance company operating in Country Z. Company D specialises in Income Protection (IP) business. Over the last few years, Company D has increased its market share significantly.
Company D's expense ratio is defined as follows:
Total expenses (initial, renewal, termination, claim and investment)
Premiums earned
Company D's annual financial statements are showing an upward trend in the expense ratio, which is now above the sector average in Country Z, and the local insurance regulator has expressed concerns about this upward trend.

i.question: Suggest possible reasons why the regulator may be concerned with this upward trend.

Indicative Solution:
The trend could adversely affect policyholders' security due to the company's reduced ability to meet its contractual obligations to existing policyholders.
An increasing expense ratio may indicate that the insurer's expenses are growing faster than its premiums and investment income. This trend may lead to reduced profitability, potentially impacting the insurer's financial stability.
Higher expenses could also put upward pressure on premium rates, and higher new premium rates could result in lower new business volumes leading to higher expense ratio and lower solvency position.
An increasing expense ratio may signal operational inefficiencies within the insurer which could lead to service quality issues, which could adversely affect persistency experience and solvency position.
An increasing expense ratio may lead to a reduction or deferral of the company's tax liability, and the regulator would want to ensure that the company's tax position has not been manipulated by management.

ii.question: Suggest possible reasons for this upward trend.

Indicative Solution:
General observations:
The increasing trend in “Total expenses” could be caused by the increasing trend of any of its components including initial, renewal, termination, claim, and investment expenses.
Alternatively, the mix could have altered towards a higher number of lower premium policies, which results in lower contributions towards overhead expenses.

Initial expenses:
Company D could have increased its development costs significantly over the years to drive new business sales. These could include product development costs, as well as marketing and advertising costs.
Company D could also have developed more expensive distribution channels / increased commission levels.

Renewal expenses:
The upward trend of renewal expenses could be a result of overhead expenses increasing over the years. This could be the result of investment in additional office space, more advanced underwriting, admin and claims systems and other project costs.
The increase could be driven by third party admin expenses if Company D makes significant use of outsourcers. The agreed increases in inflation rates / third party admin costs in the contracts may have outpaced the market / pricing assumptions.
The staffing costs could have risen due to increased team sizes / increased inefficiency / excess salary rises.

Termination / Claims expenses:
Termination and claims expenses are likely to be significant for IP business due to the size of claims in payment.
Similar to renewal expenses, there could have significant development costs in the termination and claims processes.
The costs of rehabilitation benefits and other measures that Company D uses to encourage claimant to return to work could have increased significantly.

Investment expenses:
The increase in investment expenses could be a result of changing investment strategy, e.g. unlisted / higher risk / non-vanilla assets, or an increasing costs of internal investment staff costs or external investment management.

Premium earned:
The increasing trend of premium earned is slower than the increasing expense trend, which could be a result of offering more competitive premium rates to attract new business. This could be the result of inadequate expense assumptions being used for pricing the premium rates.

iii.question: Suggest possible actions than Company D could take to improve its expense ratio.

Indicative Solution:
General observations:
As identified from (ii) above, the key actions to improve the expense ratio is to slow down the increase in “Total expenses” relative to the increase in “Premium earned”.
Improving the trend in “Total expenses” could be actioned and achieved through improving any of its components including initial, renewal, termination, claim and investment expenses.

Initial expenses:
Company D could reduce level of marketing and advertising activities if there is scope to do so.
Assess the performance of distribution channels and stop using those that underperform. (e.g. not achieving sufficient new business sales or consistently having bad persistency records).
Could adopt different commission structure (e.g. regular commission rather than upfront commission) to help spread initial expenses and improve persistency experience.
Could reduce underwriting costs by making use of services and support provided by reinsurers.
Could implement automated underwriting systems to reduce the time and resources required for assessing risks.

Renewal expenses:
Reduce admin costs through process improvement exercises.
Assess the costs and benefits of existing third-party admin arrangements and re-negotiate more appropriate contract terms if necessary.
Regularly review and update pricing assumptions to ensure that they keep up with the pace of the agreed increases in inflation rates / third party admin costs in the contracts.
Upgrade and optimise technology systems to improve overall efficiency and reduce ongoing operational costs, although there would be a one-off upfront cost impact, which would adversely impact the ratio in the short term.
Implement digital platforms for policy administration, customer service, and claims management.
Invest in customer service training and technology to improve efficiency and customer satisfaction.
Company D could reduce cost base by reducing staff numbers if the company is over staffed.

Termination / Claims expenses:
Similar to renewal expenses, assess the costs and benefits of existing third-party admin arrangements and re-negotiate more appropriate contract terms if necessary.
Implement measures to encourage claimant to return to work and re-design if necessary.
Implement advanced claims processing systems to help improve efficiency.
Utilise data analytics to identify fraudulent claims and reduce processing time.
Provide training to claims staff to enhance their skills and productivity.

Investment expenses:
Adopt more cost effective investment management processes.
For assets used for backing liabilities, assess whether an active investment strategy to try maximise investment gains is the most appropriate approach, as a more passive investment approach aiming to match assets and liabilities closely could also reduce capital requirements in addition to reduction in investment management costs.

Premium earned:
Assess whether the allowance for pricing and profit margins in the premium rates is adequate. If not, premium rates should be increased to address the issue.
In the assessment of implications on overall profitability, the implications on new business volumes as a result of any premium increases should be taken into consideration. This is because if the benefits gained from the increase in premium rates are outweighed by the reduction in new business sales, overhead expenses will be spread over a lower number of policies and thus introduce limited effect on improving the expense ratio.

Other measures
Consider outsourcing non-core functions to reduce in-house operational costs.
Conduct regular expense audits to identify areas for improvement.
Establish a continuous improvement culture/efficiency within the organisation.

iv.question:
The regulator has been provided with Company D's most recent analysis of expense experience. It further requested that Company D explains how the following expense assumptions were derived for pricing and statutory purposes:

• initial expenses
• renewal expenses
• termination expenses
• claims expenses
• investment expenses.

Explain how the pricing and valuation expense assumptions could be derived using Company D's expense analysis.

Indicative Solution: The starting point of setting the expense assumptions will be Company D's budget. This is likely to be next year's budget based on Company D's business plan including management's target new business sales. The budget will include all the salary related, operational and business management costs, split by functions/departments. It is possible that more than 1 year's future budget can be used, but budgeting for a longer than 1 year time horizon will increase the level of planning uncertainties.

Initial expenses:
Initial commissions will be based on the terms agreed between Company D and its distribution channels. These are normally expressed as a percentage of premium for each new policy written, and therefor can be loaded on to the new business premiums directly.
For one-off projects, business development and marketing costs, Company D will need to determine an appropriate time horizon to spread the cost, and then load on to the new business premiums taking into consideration projected new business volumes.
Underwriting and new business costs are likely to be expressed as fixed costs for each new business, where the fixed costs per policy will be derived taking into consideration projected new business volumes.
For valuation purposes, initial expense assumption relating to initial expenses that incur at the point of sale will not be needed.
If the initial commissions are on an indemnity basis, any commission clawback allowance due to early lapses will need to be allowed for in both pricing and valuation.

Renewal expenses:
Renewal commissions will be based on the terms agreed between Company D and its distribution channels. These are normally expressed as a percentage of premium for each new policy written and therefore can be loaded on to the new business premiums directly.
For overhead expenses such as admin, finance, management and operational costs, where these are not outsourced, a per policy expense / unit cost assumption will be derived. The per policy expense assumption will be derived using the budgeted total maintenance costs divided by the total number of policies. The total number of policies will include all the policies in force at the expense investigation date plus expected new business in accordance with the new business plan.
For valuation purposes, depending on the local regulatory requirements, a prudence margin may be added to the best estimate per policy expense assumption.
For services that are outsourced, the per policy expense assumption will be directly linked to the third-party fees agreed between Company D and the third party service providers. A prudence margin is unlikely to be needed as there is no uncertainty in the estimation of contractual third-party service fees.
Expense inflation relating to renewal expenses will need to be estimated. This could be based on that assumed in the business plan budget, which could be based on price inflation, earnings inflation or a blend of both. The expense inflation assumption should be consistent with Company D's other financial assumptions. Similar to per policy expense assumption, a prudence margin may be added to the best estimate expense inflation assumption.

Terminal and Claims expenses
For termination and claims expenses that are not outsourced, a per termination / claim expense assumption will be derived. The per termination / claim expense assumption will be derived using the projected total termination / claim costs divided by the total number of terminations / claims. The total number of claims will include existing claims in payment plus additional projected future claims as per the business plan. However, the total number of terminations will include future terminations only as unlike claims in payment terminations are one-off events.
For claims services that are outsourced, the claims expense assumption will be directly linked to the third-party fees agreed between Company D and the third party service providers.
Claims expense inflation relating to renewal expenses relating to both pricing and valuation could be estimated similar to that for renewal expense inflation assumption.

Investment expenses:
For investment management services that are not outsourced, this will be based on the total costs of Company D's investment management function plus any relevant overhead expenses. The investment management expenses could include fees relating to dealing/ transaction costs, custodial fees, hedging fees, valuation fees and other investment-related expenses.
The investment management expenses could then be derived using the total investment costs divided by total value of assets under management, which would be expressed as a percentage (or basis points) of the asset value.
For valuation purposes, a prudence margin may be added to allow for the uncertainty underlying the best estimate investment expense assumption.
For investment management services that are outsourced to external asset management companies, the investment management fees are already known. As such, the investment expense assumption will link directly to the investment management fees agreed between Company D and the external investment management companies, and there will be no need to add prudence margin for valuation purposes.

4.label: Question 4
4.scenario: Company A is a large health and care insurer in Country X, specialising in Long-Term Care (LTC) insurance business. Company A has the largest market share in Country X's LTC insurance sector and is regarded as one of the leading brands in the country. Company A's product range includes both the pre-funded and immediate needs LTC plans.

i.question: Discuss the possible advantages and disadvantages of a pre-funded LTC plan from the perspective of policyholders.

Indicative Solution:
Advantages from the perspective of policyholders
The product provides peace of mind in knowing that any LTC requirements are catered for.
Premiums are paid over a long period of time (while they are still young and healthy) hence the absolute amount of premiums is likely to be lower than the once-off lump sum required under immediate needs products. This makes the product more affordable for the customers as it helps spread the costs and does not require a lump sum premium payment.
For long term pre-funded LTC, the customer may be able to get a surrender value if they are forced to cancel the policy after years of payment. There may even be a paid-up value if they stay healthy beyond the expected LTC claims date.
The product could be infused with pension benefits such that the customers may be able to draw down on benefits after retirement even before LTC is triggered.
Premiums could be attractive under a high interest rate/investment return economic condition.
The pre-funded feature also helps encourage customers to seek protection for the future, maybe at a time when the customers are still working and thus be able to afford the premium payments, as well as being able to afford taking out additional benefit such as the assistive devices required to adapt to the life after LTC is triggered.
The provision of independent care advice at the point of sale helps the policyholders understand the care choices available to them and their rights to State support in advance.

Disadvantages from the perspective of policyholders
Customer is paying in advance for cover but is uncertain whether they will ever get any benefit from the product, customer does not know what their circumstances will be many years in the future and whether LTC will be appropriate for them.
The customer does not know if the benefits paid out will be sufficient to meet the cost of long term care many years in the future.
Surrender values and paid-up values are usually a proportion of the premiums paid to date therefore the customer may suffer some loss upon cancellation.
If the customers do not have conditions which trigger benefit payment, they may not be able to utilise the benefit which then looks like a loss to the customer.
If the policy does not have death benefits, the customer may die before they could utilise the long term care benefits.
Given the long term nature of LTC products, it is unlikely to be a priority on the younger customers' insurance list.
Premiums could be high or even unaffordable under a low interest rate/investment return economic condition.

ii.question:
Company A is in the process of assessing the effectiveness of its risk management, governance and control operations.

Suggest the possible operational failures and frauds that Company A may encounter in its normal day-to-day operations.

Indicative Solution:
Underwriting personnel may make errors in underwriting such that riskier lives are accepted when they should have been declined or charged a lower premium than prescribed by the underwriting manual.
Claims personnel may make errors such that claims are paid for customers who should not have been paid based on the claims procedure manual. Such errors may include: paying customers who are still on a waiting period; overpaying customers beyond the benefit limit; not meeting payment deadlines with service providers like care homes.
Customer onboarding teams may input customer data incorrectly leading to poor data quality.
Systems may be breached or penetrated by external third parties leading to loss of data.
Internal employees may also share confidential data with third parties without authorisation and exercising due care.
Customer experience team may fail to address customer requests in accordance with policy contractual terms leading to reputational damage to the company.
Payments may be made to wrong account numbers resulting in losses if the money cannot be recovered and client complaints from claimants who should have been paid.
Employees may fraudulently create policies and customers and claim for those fake policies.
Employees may also deliberately underpay benefits for claimants and pocket the differences.
Poor expense controls and purchasing controls may result in employees influencing service providers to overstate invoices so that employees share the differences with service providers.
Passwords to key models and data records may be lost thus resulting in inability to access key information at the required times.
The company may erroneously continue making payments to past claimants who would have already died.
Lack of finance authorisation controls may result in employees siphoning funds out of the company.
Errors in models may result in the company reporting incorrect financial results.
System weaknesses may result in manipulation of company data and possible financial losses to the company.
Weak internal processes may result in the company not recovering claims from reinsurers.
Brokers may fraudulently keep premiums collected from customers without passing on to the company.
Sales teams ignore sales controls leading to mis-selling.
Company may accidently invest in the wrong funds or assets.
Fraudulent claims from policyholders who are not in ill health.

iii.question:
In the past 6 months, Company A has declined a number of claims on pre-funded LTC plan policies. Company A's administration system had identified the policyholders as having already claimed, and regular claim payments were being made to their respective care homes.
The affected policyholders have complained to the regulator about being treated unfairly. The story has also captured a significant amount of attention on Country X's social media, damaging Company A's brand image.

Discuss the initial investigations that Company A should undertake.

Indicative Solution:
Process investigations
Inspect the integrity of the claims payment process.
It is possible that the claim payments were actually being made as part of a wide internal fraud syndicate. Therefore, it is critical to investigate whether payments actually occurred and what was the trail of payments made.
For all the claims in question, the company should use the bank statements to identify whether payments were actually made.
The company should investigate whether the erroneous claims reflected in the system have actual physical trails/records supporting them. This includes investigating if claims forms had indeed been submitted.
For each claim, identify which staff processed the claim forms, and which staff authorised the claim payments.
It is also important to check if customers acknowledged submitting the claim forms so that if any forging of signatures by internal personnel occurred, it can be ascertained.
For submitted claims, inspect evidence of claim assessments by the insurer's doctor, policyholder's doctor and independent medical adviser should be sought to determine if any incorrect decisions have been made.
Obtain information relating to the bank account details to which the claims payments were made, and conduct investigation to identify and trace the holders of those bank accounts.
Investigate to decide whether the issues were caused by genuine errors and fraudulent activities. If there is sufficient information to suggest fraudulent activities, police may be consulted, so that they may perform formal investigations and bring criminal charges against those responsible for committing the frauds.
Check whether the annual membership and benefit statements being sent to customers contain the incorrect information or the correct information of no claims. If the correct information was being sent to customers, the company must investigate why correct benefit statements were run despite the fraudulent claims being made. If the incorrect information was being sent to customers, the company must investigate why customers did not notify the companies sooner.
Investigate whether the care homes concerned have actually received the payments.

System Investigations
Inspect the integrity of the IT systems.
System investigations should try to identify whether there were errors, flaws or faults in the system relating to: design/functionality; governance and controls.
The investigation should aim to identify whether the issues were due to the systems or human error.
It is important to identify whether system failures were caused by the software providers, or caused by fraudulent activities committed by the company's staff, so as to avoid bringing false charges of fraud against the company's staff. Such investigation may involve inspecting whether the system randomly generate entries that were not backed by actual payments.
Investigation should also be carried out in respect of system security. More specifically, investigate whether unauthorised access has been made. If so, whether it was committed by internal staff or external parties, and whether the unauthorised access was a result of system weaknesses. i.e. systems being hacked, or confidential access information have been leaked, to unauthorised persons.
The company should carry out penetration tests to identify weaknesses that may be causing ease of data breaches and manipulations.
It is important to identify whether the system security has been compromised intentionally or due to design failure.

Financial Impacts
The company needs to quantify the financial impacts of this issue, both in terms of number of policyholders affected and total costs.
The company should investigate if there are other customers who are yet to lodge claims but may have been affected by similar issues.
The total costs will depend on whether actual claims payment have been made, or it is purely a system error.
The financial impacts should also take into consideration the potential compensations to affected policyholders, costs of investigation and remediation, any potential fines/penalties from the regulator, any potential loss in business/profits due to reputational damage.
The impact of the claim payments on company profits, reserves and capital requirements must be ascertained.

Other Investigations
Interview the policyholders affected so that their side of the story can be ascertained and compared against internal investigations.
Interview the care home management so that their side of the story can be ascertained and compared against internal investigations.

iv.question:
Company A's initial investigations confirm that the claims records for the affected policyholders were incorrect, due to a combination of human and system errors.

Suggest possible actions that Company A may take to:

• address the investigation findings.
• restore its brand image.

Indicative Solution:
Addressing the investigation findings
Controls
The company should ensure that there is a robust claim control process so that any errors made can be identified before actual claims being made.
The company should put in place systems that enable regular spot checks/audits of customer records and what is in the systems.
Implement regular spot checks/audits where receipt of claim payments are confirmed with customers for authentication.
Customers should be made aware and encouraged to check their annual membership and benefit statements, and confirm if they are matching the payments they have received or not.
The company can consider automating submission of claims and subsequent processing of individuals so as to reduce human involvement in processing of claims.
Internal audit procedures should be reviewed and updated so as to ensure any control failures or fraudulent activities can be identified early and corrective actions are implemented.
Links management performance objectives to internal audit ratings so that employees are incentivised to ensure controls are adhered to.
Procedure manuals should be reviewed and updated to ensure any risks and weaknesses identified during the investigations are mitigated.
Consequences for not following procedures and policies must be enforced and communicated to employees.
Fix all the system errors and weaknesses identified.
Replace the system and service providers if necessary.

Training
Staff must be trained on the importance of capturing data correctly and the need to have an appropriate risk culture.
Regular product knowledge and policy knowledge tests should be implemented so that the company is able to continuously test knowledge and awareness.
Refresher courses on various procedures and policies should be readily available to employees.
A central repository of procedure manuals and policies should be developed to ensure easy access by employees.
Implement user helpline to enable help is at hand if employees are encountering any system or procedural issues.
Consequences for not following procedures and policies must be enforced and communicated to employees.

Restoring the brand image
In order to restore the brand image, the company should ensure that it is being perceived to be open and transparent about the issues, and it is fully committed to address the issues for the benefits of its customers.

Affected Customers
The insurer should contact the customers affected by the errors and issue an apology to the customers.
The insurer should be open about the causes of the errors and what the company has done to correct the errors going forward.
The company should state what compensation will be offered to compensate for the financial losses, and provide explanation on how the value of compensation has been evaluated.
The company should also offer the affected customers opportunity to ask further questions or seek further assistance via a dedicated helpline.

Other customers and the public
A press release should be circulated to customers and in the media to acknowledge the issues.
The press release should summarise the findings of the investigations and how the company addressed findings and recommendations of the investigations.
The company should roll out awareness campaigns encouraging customers to check their benefit and premium statements to ensure that information is correct.
Encourage the policyholders to contact the company's general helpline if any customer identifies or suspects a policy error on their information.
A media briefing can be conducted to ensure that members of the media can ask questions and the company can respond to those questions.

Regulators
The company should provide the regulator with detailed findings and proposed actions relating to the issues, including details on how compensations provided to the affected customers have been determined to ensure that they are being treated fairly.
The company may engage an independent external party / skilled person / independent expert to perform an independent assessment of the cause and the company's proposed actions in respect of the issues.
The company may volunteer to provide the regulator with regular updates on progress made in improving controls.
The company can also detail how the scandal may have an impact on its reserves and solvency capital requirements including any adjustments to operational risk capital charges.

Shareholders
A voluntary disclosure and update should be presented to the shareholders summarising the issues and how the company has addressed any weaknesses in controls. The management information pack would place more focus on whether the company expects the issues to affect its future profitability.

5.label: Question 5
5.scenario: Country Y is a developing country, with a significant proportion of its population on low incomes. Company B is a small health and care insurance company specialising in health and care insurance products that target individuals in work on low incomes.

i.question: Suggest possible actions that the government of Country Y and Company B may take to help improve the country's lower-income groups' access to health and care insurance products.

Indicative Solution:
Actions for Company B
Design and price health and care products aimed at lower-income groups to be affordable for this market.
Carry out a market research to understand the needs of lower-income earners of the country, and ensure that the products that the company rolls out to lower-income earners are fit for purpose and meet customer needs.
Design and market products to employers whose workforce consists of significant proportion of low-income earners.
Target various affinity groups relevant in the lower-income earners' communities including churches, mosques, trade unions, business cooperatives etc, to purchase health and care product for their members.
Reduce product expenses in order to provide low-cost products whose premiums are affordable to the target market, for example, reduce distribution cost by selling products on digital platforms including on mobile phones which may be readily available to the target market.
Apply for developmental grants from organisations that support financial inclusion so as to reduce cost of capital for the products.
Devise mechanisms to collect premiums in an efficient manner e.g. automated deductions from payroll or direct debits.
Partner with aggregators who have access to low income earning communities including micro-banks and mobile network operators.
Seek reinsurance support for the products in order to reduce its risk costs.
Educate the population on the benefits of H&C insurance - e.g. explain the features of the policy

Actions for government of Country Y
Make it mandatory for employer to provide health and care products to employees on low incomes.
Require insurers to cap the charges on health and care products so they are affordable for those on low incomes.
Require insurers to product health and care products that are designed to be appropriate for those on low incomes.
Provide subsidies to cover the employee insurance costs for companies that employ a significant proportion of lower-income earners.
Provide subsidies to affinity groups.
Provide direct financial support to group health and care insurance products, such as tax exemptions for premiums and claims, and lower income tax rate for the insurer, direct subsidy on its product and other instruments.
Refine Country Y's insurance regulatory requirements to help reduce the premium rates by imposing lower level of reserving and solvency capital requirements on products offered to lower-income communities.

ii.question:
Company B wishes to sell more new business, and has approached Reinsurer C, a global reinsurance company, for financial support. This support will be in the form of a Financial Reinsurance (Fin Re) arrangement, where Reinsurer C provides a loan to Company B, and the repayments of the loan are contingent on the stream of future profits generated by Company B's future new business.

Discuss the potential advantages and disadvantages of this financial reinsurance arrangement from the perspective of Company B.

Indicative Solution:
Advantages from the perspective of Company B
This will enable Company B to write more new business - which increases the profit/market share for the company.
The increase in business volume could help Company B achieve further economies of scale and enhance the company's profitability/reputation.
As the loan repayment is contingent on profits of future new business, Company B is not committed to make any repayments unless its future new business is profitable, this is likely to mean no immediate liability or capital requirement to be created in Company B's balance sheet, while there is an immediate cash injection which will help alleviate new business strain, and Company B's liquidity/solvency positions.
Higher solvency position may help improve Company B's credit ratings, which may help the company to obtain further external financial supports.
Higher solvency position could also leads to an improvement in the company's reputation in the market which enables the company to increase sales.
The reinsurance arrangement may provide Company B with access to technical support from Reinsurer C.

Disadvantages from the perspective of Company B
The company will forego future profits to the reinsurer thus limiting its earnings potential.
The regulator may not necessarily recognise the contingent loan as admissible for capital reporting purposes.
The arrangement may not be effective under accounting or supervisory regimes where credit can already be taken for the future profits, and/or where a realistic liability is to be held in respect of the loan repayments.
Given the level of risk to the reinsurer, it is reasonable to expect Reinsurer C to incorporate a profit/risk margin to reflect the risk. The ultimate cost of financial reinsurance could therefore be significantly higher compared to the cost of normal loans.
It is unclear what the tax implications would be to Company B in respect of the immediate cash injection, as well as future profits on new business and the loan repayments.
The arrangement might attract adverse attention from the regulator, which could include: the level of reliance and exposure of Company B on Reinsurer C's financial support; if part of the capital raised is used (either directly or indirectly) by Company B to finance dividends to shareholders; the cost of loan represents a significant part of Company B's future financial commitments; the arrangement introduces additional legal and third-party risks.

iii.question:
As part of the negotiation process, Reinsurer C has requested that Company B provide information on how it incorporates climate change into its business management.

Suggest possible reasons why Reinsurer C is interested in Company B's climate change strategy.

Indicative Solution:
As the loan repayment is contingent on profits of future new business, Reinsurer C would want Company B to write profitable future new business.
Company B's climate change policy could affect its brand name / public image, and have a direct implications on its ability to attract new customers and hence write profitable new business.
Climate change policy could also affect Company B's business decision making, strategic planning and investment strategy which could also affect profitability of its business.
Reinsurer C may already have a committed climate change policy, and consider it to be ethically right to partner with insurers who also value climate change strategy as part of their business change strategy.
Reinsurer C may also not want to be perceived to be associated with insurers who are deemed to increase climate change instead of damaging its reputation in the market.
Reinsurer C may simply be complying with local regulatory requirements.
The regulator may have placed capital charges on any insured risks that increase climate change, and hence the reinsurer would need to factor climate change strategy of cedants in their capital adequacy assessments.

iv.question:
Describe how Company B can allow for the impacts of climate change in its:

• product design.
• pricing.
• reserving and capital management.
• risk management.
• investment.

Indicative Solution:
Product Design
Products could be designed in line with the objectives of the UN Sustainable Development Goals.
Products provided by the insurer should not exacerbate climate change but actively assist in facilitating better climate change mitigation, adaptation and resilience by communities.
Perils that may have been previously not covered in the past may need to be covered after considering that target markets are now significantly harmed by the perils as a result of climate change.
Products may also deliberately increase access to health and care insurance to communities significantly affected by climate change to support better community resilience.
Product development policies should require that product specifications to explicitly state how climate change considerations have been made during the product design process.
Distribution channels which involve printing of paper may be avoided in favour of paperless processes.

Pricing
The company should monitor changes in claims experience due to events that are caused by climate change including economic recessions due to droughts or floods. Any implications on claims experience due to climate change should be factored into pricing. This could include any potential changes to sickness rates and critical illness incidence rates as a result of climate.
Climate change policy could also affect a company's expenses assumptions due to extra costs to implement and maintain climate change policy.
It could also affect investment return assumptions due the change in investment strategy to avoid investing companies that cause harm to the environment.
The insurer can actively review its pricing to ensure that risks and customers who do not actively manage climate change risks have higher premiums to reflect the increase in climate change risks.

Reserving and capital management
The insurer should periodically assess whether reserving assumptions need to be adjusted to reflect changes in claims experience, lapses and morbidity due to climate change.
An insurer's internal capital adequacy model could incorporate a climate change pillar with appropriate capital charges for exposure to climate change risks or providing insurance to customers who contribute to climate change.
The reserving policy of the insurer may explicitly require that the actuaries consider the climate change risks and their impact on reserves as part the annual financial reporting requirements.
The insurer may deliberately seek sustainability certifications so that it can access cheaper financing from institutions that provide green finance facilities, this will help to reduce the cost of capital for the insurer.

Risk Management
The insurer should identify the emerging risks that the company is exposed to and are as a result of climate change.
The insurer could incorporate climate risk management into its enterprise risk management framework so that it's a stand-alone pillar given appropriate importance by the company.
Departments may be requested to review their risk indicators and incorporate climate change into their annual risk assessment processes.
Business continuity processes should incorporate emergencies caused by climate change hazards and be tested in terms of whether the company can continue to serve customers in case of such emergencies.
Employees and the Board must be adequately trained, including refresher courses, on how climate change may impact the company and how they can actively support the climate risk management strategy of the company.
Investment in and support initiatives which propose climate change adaptation and mitigation.
The company could adopt a clear climate change policy in its business operation process such as paperless underwriting and claims processes, investment in environmentally friendly companies/industries etc.
The company could make it clear its stance on climate change by disclosing its policy and commitment in its public documents such as the annual accounts.

Investments
Invest in assets which are climate friendly and facilitate a just transition to a green economy.
Ensure the insurer's investment policy statements/policies incorporate climate change considerations so that they become part of governance.
Develop ESG-related tools, metrics, and analyses into their internal and statutory investment reporting.
Require investment service providers (such as financial analysts, consultants, brokers, research firms, or rating companies) to integrate ESG factors into evolving research and analysis.
Require investment managers to undertake and report on ESG-related engagement.
Reduce investments in assets which increase climate change include carbon fuels.
